1: A type: The four chariots emerging from between the bronze mountains — YHWH's executive agents deployed through all the earth — are the type of Christ's supremacy over all cosmic powers. 'He is before all things, and in him all things hold together' (Colossians 1:17). The chariot-teams that patrol and execute YHWH's purposes throughout the four compass points are the type of Christ's ultimate authority: 'All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me' (Matthew 28:18).
2: A type: The red and black chariot-horses (v2-3) recall Revelation 6:2-8's four horsemen, where the same chariot-vision is developed in the NT's apocalyptic form. Christ as the rider on the white horse (Revelation 6:2; Zechariah 19:11) is the ultimate fulfillment of Zechariah's divine-warrior vision: the one who goes out to defeat all enemies and establish the Kingdom of God. The four chariots of Zechariah are the OT template for the four horsemen of Revelation.
3: A type: The powerful dappled horses sent south are the type of Christ's sovereign governance over the southern kingdoms (Egypt/Africa). The universal scope of the chariots' deployment — north, south, east, west — is the universal scope of Christ's authority over all nations: 'Ask of me, and I will make the nations your heritage, and the ends of the earth your possession' (Psalms 2:8, applied to the Son).
4: A theme: The question about the four chariots before their identification models the prophetic posture: seeking understanding before receiving commission. Christ teaches his disciples to ask, seek, and knock; the answers come to those who ask. The interpretive dialogue of Zechariah's visions models the community of inquiry that gathers around the risen Christ and his Spirit-given revelation.
5: A direct revelation: The four winds/spirits of heaven (arba ruchot hashamayim) that go out from standing before the Lord of all the earth are the divine executive agents. In the NT, the sevenfold Spirit (Revelation 1:4; Zechariah 5:6: the seven spirits of God sent out into all the earth) is Christ's executive agent carrying out his purposes. The transition from four winds (Zechariah) to seven spirits (Revelation) reflects the completeness of the Spirit's work in the Christ-era.
6: A type: The deployment north (against Babylon) and south (against Egypt) — the two poles of world-power — is fulfilled in Christ's defeat of all world-powers at the cross. 'He disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, by triumphing over them in him' (Colossians 2:15). The specific direction 'north' for the dominant threat is the NT's 'Babylon' (1 Peter 5:13: the church in Babylon), the spiritual equivalent of human empire's opposition to God's kingdom.
7: A type: The powerful horses going out to patrol the earth — eager for action, commissioned and deployed — are a type of the apostolic mission. 'Go into all the world and proclaim the gospel to the whole creation' (Mark 16:15). The eagerness of the chariots ('they were impatient to go') mirrors the apostles' eagerness after Pentecost: the fire of the Spirit sending them out to every direction of the earth.
8: A direct revelation: The black horses going north 'set YHWH's Spirit at rest in the north country' — the divine wrath against Babylon is satisfied. Christ's atonement is the definitive satisfaction of YHWH's wrath: 'He is the propitiation for our sins' (1 John 2:2). The northern campaign's completion — divine wrath set at rest — is the shadow of the cross's completion: 'It is finished' (John 19:30). The Spirit set at rest in the north is the Spirit that rests on Christ's completed sacrifice.
9: A theme: The oracle about the crown-making (vv9-15) comes immediately after the completion of the eight visions — divine revelation always moves toward commissioning. Christ receives all authority after his resurrection (Matthew 28:18) and immediately commissions the disciples: the completion of the atoning work issues in the apostolic mission. The pattern of vision → oracle → commission in Zechariah's structure mirrors the pattern of crucifixion → resurrection → commissioning in the Gospels.
10: A theme: The silver and gold brought from the diaspora (Babylon) for the crown is a type of the treasures of the nations brought to Christ. The Magi bring gold, frankincense, and myrrh from the east to the newborn King (Matthew 2:11). Revelation's final vision: 'The kings of the earth will bring their glory into [the new Jerusalem]' (Revelation 21:24). The diaspora's gifts to the crown of the high priest are the first fruits of the nations' ultimate tribute to Christ.
11: A direct revelation: The crown placed on Joshua the high priest's head is the central symbolic act of Zechariah's entire book — the high priest crowned is the messianic priest-king. The NT's most direct application: 'On his head are many diadems' (Revelation 19:12). Christ the risen, exalted high priest is also the crowned King: 'The LORD said to my Lord, Sit at my right hand' (Psalms 110:1, applied to Christ throughout the NT). Joshua crowned is the OT type; Christ enthroned is the eternal reality.
12: A direct revelation: 'Behold, the man whose name is the Branch — he will branch out from his place and he will build the temple of the LORD.' This is the book's climactic messianic prophecy. The Branch (Tsemach) who branches out (yitzmach) from his own place — not from royal courts or established power but from an unlikely source (Bethlehem, Nazareth, a carpenter's family) — is identified in the NT as Jesus. 'He came to his own, and his own people did not receive him' (John 1:11). He builds the ultimate temple: 'I will build my church' (Matthew 16:18); 'You yourselves, like living stones, are being built up as a spiritual house' (1 Peter 2:5).
13: A direct revelation: 'He shall bear royal honor, and shall sit and rule on his throne. And there shall be a priest on his throne, and the counsel of peace shall be between them both.' This verse is the book's Christological peak: the one who rules as king is also a priest on his throne — the priest-king synthesis impossible in the OT but fulfilled in Christ. Hebrews 7-10 is the extended exposition of this verse: Christ as 'a priest forever after the order of Melchizedek' (Hebrews 7:17) who sits 'at the right hand of the throne of the Majesty in heaven' (Hebrews 8:1), making perpetual intercession (Hebrews 7:25) while reigning as King of kings (Revelation 19:16).
14: A type: The crown deposited in the temple as a memorial pointing to the coming priest-king is a type of all the OT types and shadows that point forward to Christ. 'These are written so that you may believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God' (John 20:31). The memorial crown in the temple is the OT Scripture as a whole: 'You search the Scriptures because you think that in them you have eternal life; and it is they that bear witness about me' (John 5:39).
15: A direct revelation: 'Those who are far off shall come and help to build the temple of the LORD.' The Gentiles who build the messianic temple are the NT church. 'You are no longer strangers and aliens, but you are fellow citizens with the saints and members of the household of God, built on the foundation of the apostles and prophets, Christ Jesus himself being the cornerstone... into a holy temple in the Lord' (Ephesians 2:19-21). The 'far-off' Gentiles who build the temple are the nations that Paul brings to faith: 'How can they hear without someone preaching?' (Romans 10:14).
